---
title: "Chapter 06 — 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 05 · 📈 Linear & Logistic Models](ch05.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [07 · 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels →](ch07.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Most of classical machine learning fits a model to the data and then throws the data away. This chapter covers two families that take the opposite stance in two different ways: **instance-based** methods that keep the data and reason directly from stored examples (k-Nearest Neighbours), and **probabilistic** methods that turn classification into an exercise in counting and Bayes' rule (Naive Bayes). Both are simple, fast to set up, and surprisingly competitive — which is exactly why they remain baselines you reach for before anything fancier.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Classical supervised learning · used for fast, interpretable classification baselines · the one key idea — you can classify either by *who a point resembles* (kNN) or by *how probable each label is given the evidence* (Naive Bayes).
:::

## 6.1 — k-Nearest Neighbours: classify by resemblance

The intuition is something you already do. To guess whether a restaurant is good, you ask the people whose taste is closest to yours. **k-Nearest Neighbours (kNN)** does exactly this: to classify a new point, it finds the $k$ training examples nearest to it and takes a majority vote of their labels.

What makes kNN unusual is that there is no training step in the normal sense. It is a **lazy learner** — it does no work up front beyond memorising the dataset, and defers all computation to query time. Contrast this with **eager learners** like logistic regression or decision trees, which do heavy work fitting parameters and then predict cheaply. The "model" of kNN is literally the training set.

Step by step, classifying a query point $x$ goes like this:

1. Compute the distance from $x$ to every stored training point.
2. Sort those distances and keep the $k$ smallest — the neighbourhood $N_k(x)$.
3. Take a majority vote of the labels in that neighbourhood (for regression, average their values instead).

Written compactly, the decision rule is:

$$\hat{y} = \text{mode}\big(\{\, y_i : x_i \in N_k(x) \,\}\big)$$

where $N_k(x)$ is the set of the $k$ training points closest to $x$.

Because it stores every example and draws boundaries point by point, kNN is **non-parametric**: it makes no assumption that the data follows any particular functional form. The decision boundary can be arbitrarily wiggly, bending to whatever shape the data takes. The flip side is that it needs enough data to fill out the space, and it carries the whole dataset around forever.

::: {.callout-tip}
kNN is the canonical baseline. If a heavily-tuned model can't beat a quick kNN on your problem, the extra complexity isn't earning its keep yet.
:::

## 6.2 — Distance metrics: what "nearest" means

"Nearest" is meaningless until you choose how to measure distance, and that choice quietly defines the whole classifier. The default is **Euclidean distance** — straight-line distance in feature space:

$$d(x, z) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d} (x_j - z_j)^2}$$

A common generalisation is the **Minkowski distance** with parameter $p$, which interpolates between metrics: $p=2$ gives Euclidean, $p=1$ gives **Manhattan distance** (sum of absolute differences, as if moving along a city grid):

$$d_p(x, z) = \Big(\sum_{j=1}^{d} |x_j - z_j|^p\Big)^{1/p}$$

For text or other sparse high-dimensional data, **cosine similarity** (the angle between vectors, ignoring magnitude) often works far better than straight-line distance, because it cares about *which* features fire together rather than how large they are.

The pitfall that bites everyone: distance sums over features, so a feature measured in large units **dominates**. Imagine classifying people with `age` (range ~0–80) and `salary` (range ~0–200000). The salary differences are thousands of times larger, so Euclidean distance is effectively `salary`-only — `age` never matters. The fix is to **standardise** every feature to a comparable scale (e.g. zero mean, unit variance) before computing distances. This is not optional for kNN; it is the single most common reason a kNN implementation silently underperforms.

::: {.callout-warning}
Forgetting to scale features before kNN is the classic mistake. Without it, your distance metric is secretly ranking by whichever feature happens to have the biggest numbers — not by what's actually informative.
:::

## 6.3 — Choosing k, weighting, and the bias–variance dial

The single hyperparameter $k$ controls how smooth the decision boundary is, and it is a clean illustration of the **bias–variance tradeoff** — the tension between a model that follows the training data too closely and one that smooths over real structure.

- **Small $k$ (e.g. $k=1$):** each prediction follows its single closest neighbour. The boundary is jagged and follows noise — **low bias, high variance**. A single mislabelled training point creates its own little wrong region.
- **Large $k$:** predictions average over many neighbours, smoothing the boundary — **high bias, low variance**. Push $k$ to the size of the whole dataset and every query just predicts the majority class.

So $k$ is a smoothing knob. You tune it with cross-validation (see Chapter 11), trying a range of values and keeping the one with the best validation score. A useful habit is to pick an **odd $k$** for two-class problems so votes can't tie.

A refinement is **distance weighting**: instead of every neighbour getting one equal vote, closer neighbours vote more strongly, e.g. with weight $w_i = 1/d(x, x_i)$. This makes the prediction less sensitive to the exact choice of $k$, because far-away neighbours that scrape into the top-$k$ contribute little anyway.

<svg viewBox="0 0 320 120" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <circle cx="160" cy="60" r="6" fill="currentColor"/>
  <circle cx="130" cy="50" r="5" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"/>
  <circle cx="185" cy="75" r="5" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"/>
  <circle cx="150" cy="90" r="5" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"/>
  <circle cx="60" cy="30" r="5" fill="currentColor" opacity="0.3"/>
  <circle cx="270" cy="100" r="5" fill="currentColor" opacity="0.3"/>
  <line x1="160" y1="60" x2="130" y2="50" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="160" y1="60" x2="185" y2="75" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="160" y1="60" x2="150" y2="90" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="6" y="115" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">filled centre = query · ringed = its 3 nearest · faded = ignored</text>
</svg>

Here is the whole classifier from scratch — the mechanism is just "measure, sort, vote":

```python
import numpy as np
from collections import Counter

def knn_predict(X_train, y_train, x, k=3):
    # Euclidean distance from x to every training point
    dists = np.sqrt(((X_train - x) ** 2).sum(axis=1))
    nn = np.argsort(dists)[:k]            # indices of the k closest
    return Counter(y_train[nn]).most_common(1)[0][0]  # majority vote

# self-check: a query near class "a" cluster must come back "a"
X = np.array([[0, 0], [1, 0], [0, 1], [8, 8], [9, 8], [8, 9]])
y = np.array(["a", "a", "a", "b", "b", "b"])
assert knn_predict(X, y, np.array([0.5, 0.5]), k=3) == "a"
assert knn_predict(X, y, np.array([8.5, 8.5]), k=3) == "b"
print("ok")
```

## 6.4 — The curse of dimensionality and inference cost

kNN's two great weaknesses both grow with the size of the problem.

The first is the **curse of dimensionality**. In high-dimensional space, our geometric intuitions break down: points spread out so much that *everything becomes roughly equidistant from everything else*. When the nearest and farthest neighbours are almost the same distance away, "nearest" stops carrying information, and kNN degrades toward random guessing. Concretely, to keep the same density of neighbours around a query, the data you need grows exponentially with the number of features. This is why kNN demands aggressive feature selection or dimensionality reduction (Chapter 10) before it works on wide data.

The second weakness is **inference cost**. Because all the work happens at query time, a naive prediction must compute the distance from the query to every one of the $n$ training points: that is $O(nd)$ per query. With millions of stored examples this is painfully slow. Two families of trick speed it up:

- **Space partitioning** — structures like **KD-trees** and **ball trees** pre-organise the data so that whole regions which can't contain a neighbour are pruned, giving roughly $O(\log n)$ queries in low dimensions (but they collapse back toward brute force as dimensions grow).
- **Approximate nearest neighbour (ANN)** — methods like HNSW graphs trade a tiny bit of accuracy for enormous speedups, and are what power modern vector search (Chapter 32).

The training–inference cost profile is the exact inverse of most models: trivial to "train", expensive to predict.

| Property | kNN | Typical eager model (e.g. logistic regression) |
|---|---|---|
| Training cost | ~zero (store data) | Higher (fit parameters) |
| Inference cost | High ($O(nd)$ naive) | Low (apply formula) |
| Memory at deploy | Whole dataset | Just the parameters |
| Assumptions | None (non-parametric) | Functional form assumed |
| Curse of dimensionality | Severe | Milder |

## 6.5 — Naive Bayes: classify by probability

Now the other family. Instead of asking "who does this point resemble?", **Naive Bayes** asks "given the evidence, which label is most probable?" — and answers with **Bayes' rule**:

$$P(y \mid x) = \frac{P(x \mid y)\, P(y)}{P(x)}$$

In words: the **posterior** probability of class $y$ given features $x$ is proportional to how likely those features are under that class (the **likelihood** $P(x \mid y)$) times how common the class is to begin with (the **prior** $P(y)$). Since the denominator $P(x)$ is the same for every class, we ignore it and just pick the class that maximises the numerator:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y} \; P(y)\, P(x \mid y)$$

The hard part is $P(x \mid y)$: the joint probability of *all* the features together. With $d$ features each taking many values, there are astronomically many combinations to estimate — you'd never have enough data. This is where the "naive" trick comes in.

## 6.6 — The conditional-independence assumption

The **naive** assumption is this: *given the class, the features are independent of each other.* That is bold and usually false — but it makes the math collapse beautifully. Under it, the joint likelihood factorises into a simple product of per-feature likelihoods:

$$P(x \mid y) = \prod_{j=1}^{d} P(x_j \mid y)$$

so the full classifier becomes:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y} \; P(y) \prod_{j=1}^{d} P(x_j \mid y)$$

Now each $P(x_j \mid y)$ is a one-feature quantity you can estimate just by counting. The whole model is a table of class priors and per-feature, per-class statistics — trained in a single pass over the data.

```{mermaid}
graph LR
  X[features x1..xd] --> P1[P x1 given y]
  X --> P2[P x2 given y]
  X --> P3[P xd given y]
  Pr[prior P y] --> M[multiply all]
  P1 --> M
  P2 --> M
  P3 --> M
  M --> A[argmax over y]
```

In practice the products are computed in **log-space** — $\log P(y) + \sum_j \log P(x_j \mid y)$ — because multiplying hundreds of small probabilities underflows to zero in floating point. Summing logs is numerically safe and turns the classifier into a tidy linear scoring rule.

::: {.callout-warning}
The independence assumption is almost always violated — "free" and "money" co-occur in spam, they aren't independent. Naive Bayes still classifies well because it only needs the *ranking* of class scores to be right, not the probabilities themselves. So treat its output probabilities as poorly-calibrated (often pushed toward 0 or 1); trust the predicted class far more than the confidence number attached to it.
:::

## 6.7 — Variants, smoothing, and why it dominates text

Naive Bayes comes in flavours that differ only in how they model each $P(x_j \mid y)$:

- **Gaussian NB** — for continuous features. Assumes each feature is normally distributed within each class, so you just estimate a per-class mean and variance and plug into the Gaussian density.
- **Multinomial NB** — for counts, classically **word counts** in documents. $P(x_j \mid y)$ is the probability of word $j$ in class $y$, estimated from frequencies.
- **Bernoulli NB** — for binary features (word present / absent), which also explicitly penalises words that are *absent*.

The Multinomial and Bernoulli variants are why Naive Bayes became the textbook **spam filter** and a strong **text classification** baseline. Text is extremely high-dimensional (one feature per vocabulary word) and sparse — precisely the regime where kNN's curse of dimensionality is fatal but Naive Bayes thrives, because it only ever looks at one feature at a time and trains in a single fast pass.

There is one trap: the **zero-frequency problem**. If a word never appeared in the training spam, its estimated $P(\text{word} \mid \text{spam}) = 0$, and that single zero annihilates the entire product — one unseen word vetoes the whole prediction. The fix is **Laplace smoothing** (add-one smoothing): pretend you saw every outcome $\alpha$ extra times (usually $\alpha = 1$):

$$P(x_j = v \mid y) = \frac{\text{count}(x_j = v, y) + \alpha}{\text{count}(y) + \alpha \cdot V}$$

where $V$ is the number of possible values. This guarantees no probability is ever exactly zero, so an unseen feature merely weakens a class's score instead of vetoing it.

Here is Multinomial Naive Bayes from scratch — the entire algorithm is counting plus a log-space sum:

```python
import numpy as np

class MultinomialNB:
    def fit(self, X, y, alpha=1.0):
        # X: (n_docs, n_words) word counts; y: class labels
        self.classes = np.unique(y)
        self.log_prior = {}
        self.log_lik = {}
        V = X.shape[1]                       # vocabulary size
        for c in self.classes:
            Xc = X[y == c]
            self.log_prior[c] = np.log(len(Xc) / len(X))
            counts = Xc.sum(axis=0) + alpha  # Laplace smoothing
            self.log_lik[c] = np.log(counts / counts.sum())  # P(word|c)
        return self

    def predict(self, X):
        out = []
        for x in X:
            # score each class: log prior + sum of count-weighted log P(word|c)
            scores = {c: self.log_prior[c] + (x * self.log_lik[c]).sum()
                      for c in self.classes}
            out.append(max(scores, key=scores.get))
        return np.array(out)

# self-check: two obviously separable "documents"
X = np.array([[3, 0], [0, 3], [2, 1], [1, 2]])  # word counts
y = np.array(["a", "b", "a", "b"])
m = MultinomialNB().fit(X, y)
assert m.predict(np.array([[4, 0]]))[0] == "a"   # word-0 heavy -> class a
assert m.predict(np.array([[0, 4]]))[0] == "b"   # word-1 heavy -> class b
print("ok")
```

## 6.8 — When these methods shine and when they fail

Both families are baselines first, and knowing their failure modes is what keeps you from misapplying them.

**kNN shines** when you have low-to-moderate dimensions, plenty of data, a meaningful distance metric, and a decision boundary too irregular for a simple parametric model. It **fails** in high dimensions, on large datasets where query latency matters, and whenever features aren't scaled or the distance metric doesn't reflect true similarity.

**Naive Bayes shines** when features are many, sparse, and roughly conditionally independent — text, spam, document tagging — and when you need something that trains in one pass, handles tiny datasets gracefully, and runs instantly. It **fails** when features are strongly correlated (it double-counts the shared evidence and becomes overconfident), when you need well-calibrated probabilities, or when the true decision boundary needs feature interactions it structurally cannot represent.

The shared theme: both are cheap, transparent, hard to overfit in their basic form, and excellent sanity checks. Reach for them first; reach for heavier machinery only once they've shown you the floor.

## 6.x — Key takeaways

- **kNN** is a lazy, non-parametric classifier: store the data, then classify a query by majority vote of its $k$ nearest neighbours. No training, expensive inference.
- **Always scale features** before kNN — distance is dominated by large-magnitude features otherwise. This is the most common kNN mistake.
- **$k$ is a bias–variance dial**: small $k$ overfits (jagged boundary), large $k$ oversmooths. Tune by cross-validation; distance-weighting softens the choice.
- The **curse of dimensionality** cripples kNN in high dimensions (everything becomes equidistant); KD-trees and approximate nearest-neighbour search address its inference cost.
- **Naive Bayes** applies Bayes' rule with a "naive" conditional-independence assumption that factorises the likelihood into a product of per-feature terms — trained by counting in one pass.
- Use **log-space** to avoid underflow and **Laplace smoothing** to avoid the zero-frequency problem that would otherwise veto a prediction.
- Pick the variant by feature type: **Gaussian** (continuous), **Multinomial** (counts/text), **Bernoulli** (binary); Naive Bayes is a famously strong, fast text and spam baseline despite its false independence assumption.

## 6.y — See also

- **Chapter 04 — Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering** (feature scaling, which kNN depends on)
- **Chapter 05 — Linear & Logistic Models** (the eager, parametric counterparts)
- **Chapter 10 — Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning** (the cure for kNN's curse of dimensionality)
- **Chapter 11 — Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection** (how to choose $k$ and compare baselines)
- **Chapter 12 — Bayesian Machine Learning** (priors, posteriors, and probabilistic modelling done fully)
- **Chapter 28 — Natural Language Processing** (where Multinomial Naive Bayes earns its keep)
- **Chapter 32 — Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search** (approximate nearest-neighbour search at scale)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 05 · 📈 Linear & Logistic Models](ch05.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [07 · 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels →](ch07.html)
